trajectory_summary: The call starts warm — Kenneth returned the call and asked about plans — but Joe never converts that warmth into a locked commitment. Kenneth's utility rant at 03:58 is the call's energy peak and goes unanchored. The call ends with Kenneth closing it himself on an open-ended 'I'll get back to you,' leaving the outcome entirely in the customer's hands.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is unknown, but the observable outcome is a tentative callback with no locked time, no button_up elements completed, and bill photo not yet received. This should be classified as a conditional/fragile follow-up, not a booked appointment. Per the codex's soft_outcome_distinction directive, 'I'll get back to you' without a locked time is a materially different outcome from a scheduled callback.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Conditional close — locking a time anchor when button_up can't complete now
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I sent you a text message, and you can just reply to that with the picture of the graph. And then the very last step is we we would just schedule, like, another phone call to fill over the the numbers and stuff in a bit more detail. And you have a crazy schedule, so, yeah, we we would work around that.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Joe had a concrete anchor in hand — 'back in Hampton tomorrow' — and didn't use it. The call ends with zero scheduling commitment. On a paid lead, this is a missed sit. The coaching move is specific: drill the conditional close pattern ('You're back in Hampton tomorrow — does 2pm or 4pm work?') so Joe has a tool for the next time a customer's schedule is the obstacle rather than the reason to give up.

key_moments.quote: the qualifications are a little bit different from from last time now. You just need to be on the title of the home and have a credit score of 670.
key_moments.stewart_read: Joe states the credit and title requirements as program-pitch content but never actually collects Kenneth's answers. He doesn't ask 'Are you on the title?' or 'Is your credit above 670?' — he recites the thresholds and moves on. The codex marks address_homeowner_confirmed as hard_dq_if_missing: not_homeowner. This is a verify completion gap: the follow-up sit is being built on unconfirmed qualification.

key_moments.quote: things just started, you know, getting to the point where they're, you know, getting, you know, in the $1.70 to, you know, $1.80 range. You know, it's supposed to be a budget plan, but they're doing all this fuel stuff or whatever.
key_moments.stewart_read: Kenneth volunteers specific rate pain — his budget plan is climbing toward $1.80/kWh with fuel adjustments he didn't sign up for. Joe's response is to ask which utility company. That's useful data collection, but it skips the emotional flip entirely. The codex's capture pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip' — Joe does ask (implicitly, by receiving the info) and moves to acknowledge-adjacent territory, but the flip never comes.

key_moments.quote: what what what what doubles, you know, other than, you know, you wanna build all your data centers and you wanna pay for it. You already made it. You already you you shut green solar down our our throat. You're making us pay for wind turbines that we don't want.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the call's highest-energy moment. Kenneth is not venting at Joe — he's venting at Dominion. He's doing the emotional work of the flip himself. Joe's 'Mhmm' is the single most costly response in the call: it acknowledges without anchoring, and the energy dissipates. The codex's objection_inversion mechanic is designed precisely for this moment — the customer has already identified the enemy; the rep's job is to name solar as the exit.

key_moments.quote: I'd look at both options, you know, buying outright and then versus the lease.
key_moments.stewart_read: Immediately after his utility rant, Kenneth self-directs toward a concrete purchase decision frame — he's not asking whether to do solar, he's asking which financing structure. This is a materially higher qualification signal than his earlier 'I still don't know that I'm ready.' Joe doesn't acknowledge the shift, doesn't anchor it, and doesn't use it to create appointment urgency. The customer has just told Joe he's evaluating, not browsing.
